pageTimings.text: Frogs are amphibians. Amphibians can live in water and on land. Frogs begin their life in water and later move onto land.
A female frog lays hundreds of eggs in water. The eggs are called frogspawn. Each egg is surrounded by clear jelly that protects it and keeps it safe.
After a few weeks, tadpoles wriggle out of the eggs. Tadpoles have tails and no legs. They breathe underwater using gills, like fish.
Slowly, the tadpole changes. Back legs appear first. Then front legs. The tail shrinks away. After about twelve weeks, it is a frog!
The young frog climbs out of the water. It can now breathe air through its lungs. It can also live on land. The whole world opens up.
Frogs are expert hunters. They have long, sticky tongues. A frog can flip its tongue out and back in less time than a blink to catch an insect.
A frog's skin is always damp. Frogs can breathe through their skin as well as through their lungs. They must never dry out.
Frogs come in amazing colours. Bright colours are a warning: I am poisonous — do not eat me! The more colourful the frog, often the more dangerous it is.
Frogs sing to find a mate. Each species has its own call. The ribbit of a tree frog, the deep croak of a bullfrog — their calls fill warm evenings around the world.
